title: Getting Started with Haven LLM Studio
excerpt: A complete walkthrough from installation to your first inference. Covers desktop, mobile, and SDK setup.
content:
Introduction

Welcome to Haven LLM Studio — your local AI inference powerhouse. This guide will walk you through everything you need to know to get Haven up and running, from initial installation to your first successful inference.

What You'll Need

Before we begin, make sure you have the following:

  • Node.js 20+ — The runtime for Haven's server and SDK
  • CMake 3.20+ — Required to build the native C++ layer
  • Git — To clone the repository and manage submodules
  • C++ Compiler — GCC 11+, Clang 14+, or MSVC 2019+

Step 1: Clone the Repository

git clone https://github.com/Architect-Brad/Haven-LLM-Studio.git
cd Haven-LLM-Studio

Step 2: Install Dependencies

npm install

This installs all Node.js dependencies including Express, React, Electron, and the build tools needed for the native layer.

Step 3: Initialize Submodules

git submodule update --init --recursive --depth 1

This downloads llama.cpp, the core inference engine that Haven wraps. The --depth 1 flag keeps the download small.

Step 4: Build the Native Layer

# For CPU-only (with AVX2 optimization)
./build.sh --avx2

# For NVIDIA GPU
./build.sh --cuda

# For AMD GPU
./build.sh --rocm

# For Apple Silicon
./build.sh --metal

The build process compiles the C++ inference engine and the N-API bridge that connects it to Node.js. This takes 5-15 minutes depending on your hardware.

Step 5: Start the Server

npm run server

You should see the startup diagnostics showing your platform, CPU cores, memory, and inference mode.

Step 6: Verify It Works

curl http://localhost:1234/health
# Expected: {"status":"ok","uptime":...,"native":true}

Next Steps

Now that Haven is running, you can:

  • Download a GGUF model and place it in ~/.haven/models/
  • Load the model via the desktop UI or API
  • Start making inference requests
  • Explore the mobile app or SDK
💡 Tip

Run ./first-run.sh instead of manual steps for an automated setup experience.

title: Understanding GPU Offloading in llama.cpp
excerpt: How layer offloading works, why it matters, and how Haven auto-detects the optimal configuration for your hardware.
content:
What is GPU Offloading?

GPU offloading is the technique of moving some or all of a model's layers from CPU memory to GPU VRAM. Since GPUs have massively parallel architectures, they can process these layers significantly faster than CPUs.

How llama.cpp Handles Offloading

llama.cpp uses a parameter called n_gpu_layers to determine how many layers to offload:

  • n_gpu_layers = 0 — CPU only
  • n_gpu_layers = 35 — Offload 35 layers to GPU
  • n_gpu_layers = -1 — Offload all layers to GPU

The Memory Equation

Each layer consumes VRAM. A rough estimate:

VRAM per layer ≈ Model Size in GB / Number of Layers × 1.2

For a 7B model with 32 layers at Q4 quantization, each layer needs about 250MB of VRAM. With 8GB VRAM, you can offload roughly 32 layers — the entire model.

Haven's Auto-Detection

Haven's optimization layer automatically detects:

  • Available VRAM on each GPU
  • Model size and layer count
  • System RAM for fallback
  • Whether the model fits entirely in VRAM

Based on this, it recommends the optimal n_gpu_layers value.

Multi-GPU Offloading

When you have multiple GPUs, Haven uses tensor splitting to distribute layers across them. The split is proportional to each GPU's VRAM:

{
  "multi_gpu": true,
  "tensor_split": [0.6, 0.4],  // 60% on GPU 0, 40% on GPU 1
  "main_gpu": 0
}

Performance Expectations

ConfigurationExpected Speed
CPU only (AVX2)2-5 tokens/sec
CPU only (AVX512)4-8 tokens/sec
Partial GPU offload10-20 tokens/sec
Full GPU offload30-60 tokens/sec
Multi-GPU50-100+ tokens/sec
⚠️ iGPU Warning

Integrated GPUs share system RAM. Haven detects this and recommends conservative layer counts to avoid OOM crashes.

title: Building a Cluster: Multi-Node Inference
excerpt: Set up a Haven cluster across multiple machines. Master/worker architecture, load balancing, and monitoring.
content:
Why Cluster Mode?

Cluster mode lets you distribute inference across multiple machines, enabling you to:

  • Run models larger than any single machine's VRAM
  • Handle concurrent requests across multiple nodes
  • Balance load based on each node's capabilities
  • Maintain high availability with automatic failover

Architecture Overview

Haven's cluster uses a master/worker model:

  • Master Node — Receives requests, routes to best worker, aggregates results
  • Worker Nodes — Execute inference, report stats via heartbeat

Setting Up the Master

export HAVEN_CLUSTER=true
export HAVEN_CLUSTER_ROLE=master
export HAVEN_CLUSTER_PORT=1235
export HAVEN_MAX_WORKERS=10
npm run server

Setting Up Workers

On each worker machine:

export HAVEN_CLUSTER=true
export HAVEN_CLUSTER_ROLE=worker
export HAVEN_MASTER_URL=ws://192.168.1.100:1235
export HAVEN_NODE_ID=worker-1
export HAVEN_NODE_NAME="GPU Node 1"
npm run server

Load Balancing Algorithm

The master routes requests using a weighted score:

score = (available_vram / total_vram) × 0.7 + (1 - is_busy) × 0.3

This ensures requests go to nodes with the most available VRAM, while also considering current load.

Heartbeat & Health Checks

  • Workers send stats every 5 seconds
  • Master marks nodes offline after 15 seconds without heartbeat
  • Offline nodes are automatically removed from routing
  • Workers auto-reconnect if the master restarts

Monitoring Your Cluster

# Check cluster status
curl http://localhost:1234/api/cluster/status

# List all nodes
curl http://localhost:1234/api/cluster/nodes

# Route inference through cluster
curl -X POST http://localhost:1234/api/cluster/infer \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"prompt":"Hello","config":{}}'
📘 Best Practice

Use machines with similar GPU capabilities for best load balancing results. Mixing a 4090 with an iGPU will cause uneven distribution.

title: Running LLMs on Your Phone
excerpt: Sub-1B models on Android. Thermal management, battery optimization, and the best models for mobile inference.
content:
Yes, Your Phone Can Run LLMs

Modern smartphones have powerful ARM processors with NEON SIMD instructions. Sub-1B models can run entirely on-device, offline, with no server needed.

Supported Models

ModelParametersSize (Q4)RAM Needed
SmolLM-360M360M~220MB~800MB
Qwen2.5-0.5B0.5B~350MB~1GB
TinyLlama-1.1B1.1B~670MB~1.5GB

How It Works

Haven's mobile app includes a native C++ inference engine compiled for Android ARM64. The engine uses:

  • NEON SIMD — Vectorized matrix operations
  • Foreground Service — Prevents Android from killing the process
  • Wake Lock — Keeps CPU from sleeping during inference

Thermal Management

Phones throttle when they get hot. Haven monitors thermal status:

  • Normal — Full speed
  • Light/Moderate — Reduced batch size
  • Severe/Critical — Throttled inference with warning

Battery Optimization

Android aggressively kills background processes. Haven prevents this by:

  1. Running as a foreground service with persistent notification
  2. Requesting battery optimization exemption
  3. Holding a partial wake lock during inference

Performance Expectations

DeviceModelSpeed
Pixel 8 (Tensor G3)SmolLM-360M8-12 t/s
Samsung S24 (Snapdragon 8 Gen 3)Qwen2.5-0.5B6-10 t/s
Mid-range (Snapdragon 7 series)SmolLM-360M4-7 t/s
✅ Fully Offline

Once the model is downloaded, all inference happens on-device. No internet connection required.

title: KDE Integration: A Native Experience
excerpt: System tray, D-Bus, KRunner, and Plasma widgets. Making Haven feel at home on Linux desktops.
content:
Haven on Linux Desktop

Haven doesn't just run on Linux — it integrates with it. The KDE integration makes Haven feel like a native Plasma application.

System Tray

Haven adds a system tray icon with quick actions:

  • Open Haven LLM Studio
  • Start/Stop server
  • Load model
  • Settings
  • Quit

D-Bus Service

Haven registers a D-Bus service at com.havenllm.Studio with methods for:

StartServer(port) → bool
StopServer() → bool
IsRunning() → bool
Infer(prompt, config) → string
GetStats() → string
LoadModel(path) → bool
UnloadModel() → bool
ListModels() → string

This means any Linux app can control Haven via D-Bus.

KRunner Plugin

Press Alt+F2 and type:

ask haven What is quantum computing?

KRunner sends the query to Haven and displays the response.

Plasma Widget

Add the Haven widget to your desktop or panel:

  • Server status indicator (online/offline)
  • Current model name
  • Tokens/sec display
  • CPU usage
  • Quick inference input

Installation

./integrations/kde/install.sh
📘 Note

KRunner requires Python 3 with dbus-next. Install with pip install dbus-next.

title: AVX2 vs AVX512 vs NEON: CPU Benchmarks
excerpt: Comprehensive performance comparison of CPU backends across different hardware platforms.
content:
CPU Inference Performance

Not everyone has a GPU. Haven optimizes CPU inference with three instruction sets: AVX2, AVX512, and ARM NEON.

Test Setup

  • Model: Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct (Q4_K_M)
  • Prompt: 50 tokens
  • Generation: 128 tokens
  • Settings: temperature=0.8, top_k=40, top_p=0.9

Results

HardwareBackendTokens/secRelative
Intel i9-14900KAVX51212.4100%
Intel i9-14900KAVX29.879%
AMD Ryzen 9 7950XAVX51211.290%
AMD Ryzen 9 7950XAVX28.972%
Apple M3 MaxNEON18.6150%
Raspberry Pi 5NEON2.117%

Key Findings

  1. AVX512 is 25-30% faster than AVX2 — The wider vector registers (512-bit vs 256-bit) make a significant difference for matrix operations.
  2. Apple Silicon dominates — The M3 Max's unified memory architecture and NEON implementation outperform even high-end x86 CPUs.
  3. Raspberry Pi is viable — At 2.1 t/s, it's slow but functional for small models and short responses.

Recommendations

  • If your CPU supports AVX512 (Zen 4+, Intel Sapphire Rapids+), always use it
  • AVX2 is the baseline for acceptable CPU inference
  • For ARM devices, NEON is mandatory — there's no fallback
⚠️ CPU vs GPU

Even the fastest CPU (18.6 t/s) is slower than a mid-range GPU (40-60 t/s). Use CPU inference when GPU is unavailable, not as a replacement.
